title: How to enable systemd in containers?
body:
Where can I get containers for running systemd inside podman? Is it only the Fedora containers from the Fedora registry?

What needs to be done to modify a container to run systemd inside it on podman? I would like to run Ubuntu.

comments.body:
Podman natively supports systemd in a container; if we detect that a container is using systemd as PID1, we will automatically add support (alternatively, you can manually enable it with the `--systemd=always` flag).

It is sufficient to take any container image that contains systemd and set the entrypoint to `/usr/sbin/init` (or whatever the path to the systemd binary happens to be).

> It is sufficient to take any container image that contains systemd

That is my question. Such containers are hard to come by - Docker doesn't support systemd. Either 1) where can I find such a container, or 2) how can I modify a typical Docker container to use systemd init?
I would imagine that you could build using a standard Ubuntu image as your base and run an `apt install` inside to grab systemd and associated dependencies?

To be clear, there should be no magic here. The container has access to the standard Ubuntu repositories, which will container systemd to service all non-container installs. Said package can be installed from a `RUN` instruction while building a container, and then you can set systemd as the entrypoint via `ENTRYPOINT`.

I can't speak more specifically because I'm a Fedora user myself (Fedora image include systemd by default, so no need to rebuild) - but figuring out what package provides systemd should not be difficult.
Thank you @mheon. I think I'm working it out.

ubi8-init
Image comes ready to run systemd as pid1.
